name: Intellia Therapeutics, Inc.

profile.description: Intellia Therapeutics is a clinical-stage genome editing company focused on developing potentially curative therapies using CRISPR/Cas9 technology. Lead programs include nexiguran ziclumeran (nex-z) for transthyretin amyloidosis (ATTR) in partnership with Regeneron, with Phase 3 MAGNITUDE trial ongoing after FDA lifted a clinical hold in March 2026. Pipeline spans in vivo and ex vivo gene editing across ATTR, hemophilia A, and other genetic diseases using proprietary lipid nanoparticle delivery. Founded in 2014, headquartered in Cambridge, Massachusetts. Post-restructuring headcount of approximately 400 following 27% workforce reduction in 2025.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): Intellia advanced CRISPR gene editing from laboratory concept to Phase 3 clinical trials, with NTLA-2001 demonstrating 87% serum TTR reduction at the highest dose in Phase 1 results published in the New England Journal of Medicine—a landmark first for in vivo CRISPR therapy [1][4]. However, the clinical hold on nex-z following a patient death (grade 4 liver adverse event) represents a significant setback, though the FDA lifted the hold in March 2026 [4]. AI Deployment (0/2): No evidence of AI-powered products shipping or AI-driven cost savings reported. Intellia's work is fundamentally wet-lab and clinical rather than computational [2]. Market Share (1/2): Intellia is a pioneer in in vivo CRISPR gene editing alongside CRISPR Therapeutics and Editas Medicine, with differentiated lipid nanoparticle delivery technology, but the market is pre-commercial with no approved products generating market share data [1]. Product Pipeline (2/2): Nex-z (Phase 3 MAGNITUDE trial for ATTR-CM, partnered with Regeneron), hemophilia A (Phase 1 IND filed), plus base editing and DNA writing programs in research stage—specific programs with clear clinical timelines [1][4]. R&D Intensity (1/1): As a clinical-stage biotech, essentially all resources are directed toward R&D, spending ~$400M annually on clinical development and research—far above any industry benchmark [3].

inspiration.label: WEAK
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): Intellia's mission to develop 'potentially curative therapies' using CRISPR gene editing for patients with serious genetic diseases is specific, distinctive, and connects the company's technology to a deeply meaningful purpose—curing diseases that currently have no treatment [2]. The purpose is clearly thought through and differentiates from traditional pharma's incremental approach. However, the company is too young (founded 2014) to have validated this through decades of execution. Community Impact (0/2): No specific charitable programs, community investment initiatives, or patient access programs identified beyond standard biotech patient engagement and corporate responsibility reporting [5]. Employee Retention (0/2): Two consecutive years of workforce reductions—15% in January 2024 and 27% net reduction in 2025—represent a pattern of organizational instability that undermines employee trust and retention [2]. The strategic reorganization to focus on late-stage programs, while pragmatic, signals financial pressure. Insider Buying (0/2): No evidence of meaningful net insider buying identified in the last 12 months.

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available growth-stock metrics (Forward P/E and PEG excluded as unavailable due to negative earnings). D/E of 13% is extremely low, well below the 70% target, reflecting minimal debt against $376M cash and $80M total debt [3]. However, ROE of -56.34% (net loss of -$395M on ~$700M equity) and deeply negative profit margin (-597%) score zero on profitability metrics [3]. Revenue growth YoY is estimated at -8.5% (FY2026 consensus of $60.4M vs. LTM of $66M), and 5-year revenue CAGR of approximately 5% falls short of the 10% target [1][3]. The company is burning $364M annually in free cash flow with only $376M remaining, providing approximately 12 months of runway before additional financing is required [3]. Weighted calculation on available metrics: D/E 1.0 + ROE 0.0(2x) + RevYoY 0.38 + Rev5yr 0.67(2x) + Margin 0.0 = 2.72/7 × 10 → 4.

ai_resilience.label: MODERATE
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (7/9): Intellia's core business—developing CRISPR gene therapies—requires wet-lab biology, proprietary lipid nanoparticle manufacturing, and FDA-regulated clinical trials that AI structurally cannot substitute [1][2]. The physical delivery of gene editing components to human cells, safety monitoring in clinical trials, and GMP manufacturing create deep moats. However, AI-enhanced drug discovery platforms at larger competitors could accelerate the development of alternative therapeutic approaches (antisense oligonucleotides, small molecules) that compete with CRISPR for the same patient populations, creating indirect competitive pressure. AI Opportunity (0/1): No AI-specific revenue contribution identified. Intellia's operations are fundamentally biological and clinical rather than computational, with no AI products or services generating revenue [2].

infinite_game.summary: Intellia's purpose—curing genetic diseases with CRISPR—is genuinely infinite in ambition, but the company's execution under financial pressure reveals finite-game behaviors. Two consecutive years of workforce reductions (15% in 2024, 27% in 2025) and strategic narrowing to late-stage programs reflect survival-mode decision-making rather than building to endure. The clinical hold on nex-z following a patient death was a crisis that tested organizational resilience, and the company responded pragmatically rather than with long-term vision.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): Curing genetic diseases with CRISPR gene editing is a genuine cause that describes a future state not yet achieved—a world where diseases like ATTR amyloidosis and hemophilia have one-time curative treatments rather than lifelong management [1][2]. The purpose is distinctive, inclusive, and service-oriented. However, the company is only 12 years old and has not yet proven the cause through a product reaching patients or surviving multi-decade execution. The recent strategic narrowing and workforce cuts suggest financial survival may be competing with the cause.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (0/2): Two consecutive years of significant workforce reductions—15% in January 2024 and 27% net reduction in 2025—have fundamentally eroded organizational trust [2]. The CFO transition announced in 2025 adds to leadership instability. Employees in a clinical-stage biotech facing successive layoffs are operating in survival mode, protecting themselves rather than advancing the cause. The strategic reorganization to focus on late-stage programs was necessary but destructive to the culture of innovation across the broader pipeline.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (0/1): No evidence found of Intellia explicitly acknowledging CRISPR Therapeutics, Editas Medicine, or Beam Therapeutics as worthy rivals whose strengths inform self-improvement. The competitive landscape is typically framed in terms of differentiation (in vivo vs. ex vivo approaches) rather than learning from rivals [1].

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (0/2): Intellia is too young (founded 2014) and too narrowly focused (CRISPR gene editing) to have demonstrated existential flexibility. The strategic pivot to focus on late-stage programs and abandon earlier pipeline was driven by financial necessity (extending cash runway) rather than a cause-driven reinvention [2]. The company has not faced the kind of strategic choice—abandoning a successful business for something new—that Sinek's framework evaluates.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Intellia has invested ~$400M annually in R&D to develop curative CRISPR therapies with no approved products and no certainty of success, burning through cash to advance science that could transform medicine [3]. The decision to continue the nex-z program after a patient death and FDA clinical hold required conviction. However, the successive workforce reductions suggest that financial pressure is driving decisions more than long-term capability building.

investment_thesis: Intellia is pioneering in vivo CRISPR gene editing with nex-z (nexiguran ziclumeran) for ATTR amyloidosis, the first-ever systemic CRISPR therapy to reach Phase 3 clinical trials. Phase 1 data published in NEJM demonstrated 87% serum TTR reduction at the highest dose, validating the proprietary lipid nanoparticle delivery platform [1]. The FDA lifted the clinical hold on the MAGNITUDE Phase 3 trial in March 2026, clearing the path for pivotal data that could support regulatory approval for a one-time curative treatment in a multi-billion dollar ATTR market [4]. The Regeneron partnership provides financial and development resources, and the pipeline extends into hemophilia A (Phase 1) and next-generation base editing and DNA writing platforms [1]. At $2.4B market cap, the stock prices in significant clinical risk but offers asymmetric upside if Phase 3 data are positive.
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on existential clinical and financial risk. The nex-z clinical hold was triggered by a patient death from a grade 4 liver adverse event, raising fundamental safety questions about the lipid nanoparticle delivery platform that underpins the entire pipeline [4]. Cash of $376M against annual burn of $364M provides only ~12 months of runway, likely requiring dilutive equity financing in 2027 at potentially distressed valuations [3]. Two consecutive years of workforce reductions (15% in 2024, 27% in 2025) have hollowed out R&D capabilities beyond the lead program, narrowing the pipeline optionality that justifies a platform biotech valuation. Revenue of $66M (entirely collaboration-dependent) with -$395M net loss and -56% ROE reflect a business with no path to standalone profitability without an approved product [3]. Competition from Alnylam's approved RNAi therapy (patisiran/vutrisiran) for ATTR gives physicians an established alternative if CRISPR safety concerns persist.
key_catalysts: MAGNITUDE Phase 3 trial data for nex-z in ATTR-CM expected to generate pivotal efficacy and safety data following FDA's March 2026 lift of clinical hold, potentially supporting regulatory filing
Hemophilia A Phase 1 IND filing and initial clinical data expanding the in vivo CRISPR platform beyond ATTR into a $12B+ therapeutic market
Base editing and DNA writing technology maturation providing next-generation precision editing capabilities beyond CRISPR/Cas9 knockdown approaches
key_risks: Nex-z safety profile remains uncertain following the grade 4 liver adverse event that triggered the FDA clinical hold—any additional serious adverse events in MAGNITUDE could permanently derail the program and the entire LNP platform
Cash runway of approximately 12 months ($376M cash, $364M annual burn) will likely require dilutive equity financing in 2027 at a time when the stock is near 52-week lows

Competition from Alnylam's approved RNAi therapies (vutrisiran) for ATTR provides physicians with a proven alternative, and the established safety profile of RNAi may limit CRISPR adoption even if nex-z demonstrates efficacy
